q: The nurse teaches that whenever you see 'thyroid,' you should change it to:
rationale: Since the thyroid gland regulates metabolism, whenever you see 'thyroid,' change it to 'metabolism.' Hyperthyroidism = hypermetabolism, hypothyroidism = hypometabolism.

q: Exophthalmos (bulging eyes) is a hallmark sign of which thyroid condition?
rationale: Exophthalmos is seen in Graves disease. 'Run yourself into the Graves disease' = hyperthyroidism with hypermetabolism, weight loss, tachycardia, heat intolerance, and bulging eyes.

q: A patient with hypothyroidism is scheduled for surgery. The nurse should question which preoperative order?
rationale: Do NOT sedate hypothyroid patients - they are already super slow. A sedative could push them into a coma (myxedema coma). Question any order for sedation in a hypothyroid patient.

q: Treatment for hypothyroidism includes Levothyroxine (Synthroid), which should be taken:
options: In the morning, 30-60 minutes before breakfast, on an empty stomach
With the largest meal of the day
rationale: Levothyroxine is taken in the morning, 30 minutes to 1 hour before breakfast, alone on an empty stomach with water.

q: Thyroid storm is a medical emergency characterized by temperatures of:
rationale: Thyroid storm features temps of 105°F or above, high BP (stroke level). Emergency treatment: bring temp down (ice packs/cooling blankets), oxygen per mask at 10L, stay with patient.

q: 'In Addison, you ADD-a-SONE' refers to treatment with:
rationale: Addison disease = undersecretion of adrenal cortex (steroids). Treatment: ADD-a-SONE = give glucocorticoids (Prednisone, Methylprednisone). Under stress, these patients can go into Addisonian crisis (shock + hypoglycemia).

q: All signs and symptoms of Cushing syndrome are the same as:
rationale: Cushing = oversecretion of adrenal cortex (steroids). All S/Sx of Cushing's are the S/E of steroid medications: moon face, buffalo hump, thin extremities, truncal obesity, hyperglycemia.

q: After a bilateral adrenalectomy for Cushing syndrome, the patient now has:
rationale: After bilateral adrenalectomy for Cushing's, the patient now has Addison disease (no adrenal cortex). Treatment: give steroids (ending in -SONE). Patient will look Cushinoid initially; takes about a year of titration to look normal.

q: Appropriate toys for a toddler (1-3 years) focus on:
options: Fine motor skills like scissors and pencils
Competitive games with winners and losers
rationale: Toddlers (1-3 years) work on gross motor skills (running, jumping). Best toys: push/pull toys (lawn mowers, wagons). They use parallel play (terrible twos).

q: The nurse is selecting toys for a preschooler (3-6 years). Which toy is most appropriate?
rationale: Preschoolers (3-6 years) work on fine motor skills with finger dexterity. They can write, draw, use colored pencils and scissors. They also work on balance (dance, tricycles). Cooperative/pretend play.

q: School-age children (7-11 years) are characterized by which '3 Cs'?
rationale: School-age (7-11 years) = Concrete, characterized by 3 Cs: (1) Creative (Legos, drawing), (2) Collective (baseball cards, Barbies), (3) Competitive (winner/losers).

q: For purposeful play (build, sort, stack), an infant should be at least:
rationale: For purposeful play (build, sort, stack, make, construct), the infant should be at least 9 months. 'Baby in womb for 9 months, another 9 months for purposeful play.'

q: The 3 Ps of nerve root compression indicating need for laminectomy are:
rationale: The 3 Ps of nerve root compression: Pain, Paresthesia (tingly), Paresis (muscle weakness). A laminectomy relieves compression by removing the vertebral spinous process.

q: The #1 answer for postoperative laminectomy positioning is:
rationale: The number 1 answer for post-op laminectomy (including any spinal surgery) is LOG ROLL. Always pick log roll for these patients.

q: A post-op lumbar laminectomy patient is at highest risk for:
rationale: Lumbar = Bladder and Legs. Lumbar innervates bladder and legs. Post-op complications: urinary retention and leg problems. Cervical = neck/arms, Thoracic = lungs/GI.

q: After laminectomy, patients should NOT sit for more than:
rationale: Post-laminectomy patients should not sit for more than 30 minutes. They can walk, stand, and lie down without restriction. Question any order for 'Up in chair for 1 hour TID.'

q: In a laminectomy with fusion, which incision site has the MOST pain?
rationale: In laminectomy with fusion, a bone graft is taken from the iliac crest (hip) and the spine. The HIP has the most pain AND the most bleeding/drainage (where the Hemovac/JP will be).

q: Temporary restrictions after laminectomy include all EXCEPT:
options: Do not sit for >30 minutes for 6 weeks
Lie flat, log roll for 6 weeks
No lifting over the head permanently
rationale: Temporary restrictions (6 weeks): no sitting >30 min, log roll, no driving, no lifting >5 lbs. PERMANENT restrictions: never lift by bending at waist, cervical - never lift over head, no jarring activities.

q: When selecting toys for children, the three considerations are:
options: Educational value, fun factor, and durability
rationale: When selecting toys: (1) Is it safe? (2) Is it age appropriate? (3) Is it feasible (possible to do easily or conveniently)?

q: Adolescents (12-18 years) in the hospital should be allowed to visit each other's rooms UNLESS:
options: One is recently post-op <12 hrs, immunosuppressed, or contagious
rationale: Allow adolescents (peer group association) to be in each other's rooms UNLESS one is: (1) Recently post-op for <12 hours, (2) Immunosuppressed, or (3) Contagious.
